title: The Little Free Library
summary: A narrator describes how a small wooden box of free books she placed on her street corner quietly transformed her neighbourhood's social fabric over the course of one year.
body: A year ago, my neighbour Tom helped me bolt a small wooden box to a post on the corner of our street. It looked like a miniature house — a tiny sloped roof, a glass door, and a hand-painted sign that read: Take a book, leave a book. We filled it with about thirty paperbacks from our own shelves, mostly novels and a few travel guides, and stepped back to see what would happen. I did not expect very much. I had been told that books would be stolen, or that the box would be vandalised, or that nobody would bother. What actually happened was quite different. Within the first week, we noticed that books were disappearing and being replaced by others. Someone had left three science fiction novels I had never heard of. Someone else had tucked in a handwritten recipe card alongside a battered copy of a French cookbook, as if they wanted to share something personal. By the end of the first month, the little library had taken on a life of its own. I started to pay attention to when people stopped at it. A teenage boy would come by most afternoons after school and spend five minutes choosing a book, sometimes putting one back and selecting another, with the same seriousness he might bring to any important decision. An older woman — I later learned her name was Margaret — had read every book in the box and replaced each one with two more. She told me it gave her something to do in the mornings that felt worthwhile. What surprised me most was how the box became a starting point for conversation. Neighbours who had lived on the same street for years began to stop and talk. I learned things about people I had never known — that the man three doors down had once been a journalist, that the young couple across the road were expecting their first child. The library had created a reason for eye contact, for pausing, for noticing each other. It was not always seamless. A few books disappeared without being replaced. Someone left a water-damaged paperback that nobody wanted. One morning I found that a child had rearranged all the books so that the spines faced inward, which I found both baffling and slightly charming. But these were minor things. The neighbourhood had quietly changed. People who once hurried past with their heads down now slowed, looked, occasionally stopped. A small wooden box had done what many larger community projects had struggled to achieve: it had made us feel that we shared something.

annotations.meaningVi: thu nhỏ, nhỏ bé theo kiểu mô hình
annotations.exampleEn: She collects miniature furniture for a doll's house.
The café had a hand-painted sign above the door.
She tucked a note in with the gift.

annotations.phrase: taken on a life of its own
annotations.meaningVi: tự phát triển, có sức sống riêng
annotations.pronunciation: /ˈteɪkən ɒn ə laɪf əv ɪts əʊn/
annotations.exampleEn: The project took on a life of its own after the first month.
I started to pay attention to how often I checked my phone.

annotations.meaningVi: cảm thấy có ý nghĩa, đáng làm
annotations.exampleEn: Volunteering made her feel that her time was worthwhile.

annotations.meaningVi: điểm khởi đầu cho cuộc trò chuyện
annotations.exampleEn: A shared interest can be a great starting point for conversation.

annotations.meaningVi: đang chờ đón đứa con đầu lòng
annotations.exampleEn: The neighbours across the road are expecting their first child.
Good eye contact shows confidence and attention.

annotations.meaningVi: trơn tru, không có trở ngại
annotations.exampleEn: The event ran seamlessly from start to finish.

annotations.meaningVi: bị hỏng do nước, bị ẩm ướt
annotations.exampleEn: The water-damaged books had to be thrown away.
It was baffling that nobody had noticed the mistake.
The books were rearranged so the spines faced inward.

annotations.meaningVi: cố gắng mà chưa đạt được
annotations.exampleEn: They struggled to achieve the targets set by the board.

annotations.meaningVi: chia sẻ điều gì đó chung
annotations.exampleEn: The event made neighbours feel they shared something.

grammarNotes.title: Past Simple Passive — Describing what was done
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng bị động quá khứ đơn để mô tả các hành động được thực hiện bởi người không xác định: 'books were disappearing and being replaced', 'I had been told that books would be stolen', 'all the books [were rearranged]'. Cấu trúc bị động (was/were + past participle) thường được dùng khi ai thực hiện hành động không quan trọng hoặc không được biết.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses past passive voice to describe actions by unknown people: 'books were disappearing and being replaced', 'I had been told that books would be stolen', 'all the books [were rearranged]'. Passive voice (was/were + past participle) is used when who performed the action is unknown or unimportant.

grammarNotes.title: Relative Clauses with 'that' and 'who' — Adding information about people and things
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài có nhiều mệnh đề quan hệ xác định: 'A teenage boy who would come by most afternoons', 'the same seriousness he might bring to any important decision', 'what many larger community projects had struggled to achieve'. 'Who' dùng cho người; 'that' hoặc bỏ đi khi là tân ngữ. Mệnh đề quan hệ giúp bổ sung thông tin mà không tạo ra câu quá dài.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses defining relative clauses: 'A teenage boy who would come by most afternoons', 'what many larger community projects had struggled to achieve'. 'Who' is used for people; 'that' can replace 'which' for things. Defining relative clauses add essential information without creating overly long sentences.

translationVi:
Một năm trước, người hàng xóm Tom của tôi đã giúp tôi bắt một chiếc hộp gỗ nhỏ vào một cái cọc ở góc phố nhà tôi. Nó trông như một ngôi nhà thu nhỏ — mái dốc nhỏ bé, cửa kính, và một tấm biển vẽ tay ghi: Lấy một cuốn sách, để lại một cuốn. Chúng tôi đặt vào khoảng ba mươi cuốn sách bìa mềm từ kệ sách của mình, chủ yếu là tiểu thuyết và vài cuốn sách hướng dẫn du lịch, rồi bước ra xa để xem điều gì sẽ xảy ra.

Tôi không mong đợi nhiều. Người ta đã nói với tôi rằng sách sẽ bị trộm, hoặc hộp sẽ bị phá hoại, hoặc chẳng ai thèm để ý. Điều thực sự xảy ra lại khá khác.

Trong tuần đầu tiên, chúng tôi nhận thấy sách đang biến mất và được thay thế bằng những cuốn khác. Ai đó đã để lại ba cuốn tiểu thuyết khoa học viễn tưởng tôi chưa từng nghe đến. Người khác đã nhét vào một tấm thẻ công thức nấu ăn viết tay cạnh một cuốn sách dạy nấu ăn Pháp cũ kỹ, như thể họ muốn chia sẻ điều gì đó cá nhân.

Đến cuối tháng đầu tiên, thư viện nhỏ đã có sức sống riêng của mình. Tôi bắt đầu chú ý đến khi nào người ta dừng lại. Một cậu bé tuổi teen thường ghé qua vào hầu hết các buổi chiều sau giờ học và dành năm phút chọn sách, đôi khi để lại một cuốn và chọn cuốn khác, với vẻ nghiêm túc mà có thể cậu sẽ dành cho bất kỳ quyết định quan trọng nào. Một người phụ nữ lớn tuổi — sau đó tôi biết tên bà là Margaret — đã đọc mọi cuốn sách trong hộp và thay mỗi cuốn bằng hai cuốn khác. Bà nói với tôi rằng điều đó cho bà điều gì đó để làm vào buổi sáng mà cảm thấy có ý nghĩa.

Điều làm tôi ngạc nhiên nhất là chiếc hộp đã trở thành điểm khởi đầu cho các cuộc trò chuyện. Những người hàng xóm đã sống trên cùng con phố nhiều năm bắt đầu dừng lại và nói chuyện. Tôi biết những điều về người ta mà trước đây tôi chưa từng biết — rằng người đàn ông cách ba căn nhà đã từng là nhà báo, rằng cặp đôi trẻ bên kia đường đang chờ đón đứa con đầu lòng. Thư viện đã tạo ra lý do để nhìn vào mắt nhau, để dừng lại, để nhận ra sự hiện diện của nhau.

Đó không phải lúc nào cũng trơn tru. Một vài cuốn sách biến mất mà không được thay thế. Ai đó để lại một cuốn sách bìa mềm bị ẩm mà không ai muốn. Một buổi sáng tôi thấy một đứa trẻ đã sắp xếp lại tất cả sách với gáy quay vào trong, điều này vừa khiến tôi bối rối vừa thấy hơi đáng yêu.

Nhưng đó là những chuyện nhỏ nhặt. Khu phố đã lặng lẽ thay đổi. Những người từng vội vã qua với đầu cúi xuống nay đi chậm lại, nhìn, đôi khi dừng lại. Một chiếc hộp gỗ nhỏ đã làm được điều mà nhiều dự án cộng đồng lớn hơn đã cố gắng mà chưa đạt: nó khiến chúng tôi cảm thấy mình chia sẻ điều gì đó chung.

questions.prompt: Who helped the narrator install the little free library?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'my neighbour Tom helped me bolt a small wooden box to a post on the corner of our street.'
questions.hint: The narrator names the person directly in the opening sentence.

questions.prompt: What did the hand-painted sign on the box say?
options: Take a book, leave a book
Little Free Library — open to all
questions.explanation: The narrator says the sign 'read: Take a book, leave a book.'
questions.hint: The sign is described as a short, balanced instruction.

questions.prompt: What were the narrator's initial expectations for the library?
options: She expected it to be very popular immediately
She did not expect very much to happen
She expected mostly children to use it
She expected it to run out of books quickly
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I did not expect very much' and lists negative predictions she had been given.
questions.hint: Think about what the narrator says after describing the setup.

questions.prompt: What unusual item did someone place alongside the French cookbook?
options: A small bouquet of dried flowers
A personal note about the book
questions.explanation: Someone 'tucked in a handwritten recipe card alongside a battered copy of a French cookbook.'
questions.hint: The object suggests the person wanted to share something personal.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe the teenage boy's approach to choosing a book?
options: He grabbed whatever was on top without looking
He chose quickly and always took the same type of book
He took five minutes, sometimes putting one back and selecting another, with great seriousness
He always brought his own books to swap first
questions.explanation: The narrator describes him spending 'five minutes choosing a book, sometimes putting one back and selecting another, with the same seriousness he might bring to any important decision.'
questions.hint: The narrator compares his book selection to making an important decision.

questions.prompt: Why did Margaret come to the little library every morning?
options: She was managing the library on behalf of the narrator
She wanted to organise the books alphabetically
It gave her something to do in the mornings that felt worthwhile
She was collecting books to donate to a school
questions.explanation: Margaret told the narrator 'it gave her something to do in the mornings that felt worthwhile.'
questions.hint: Margaret's reason is personal — she was older and found meaning in the activity.

questions.prompt: What unexpected information did the narrator learn from the conversations that began at the library?
options: That several neighbours were also keen readers
That a man nearby had been a journalist, and a young couple were expecting their first child
That many neighbours had also considered installing book boxes
That most residents had lived on the street for over twenty years
questions.explanation: The narrator learned 'that the man three doors down had once been a journalist, that the young couple across the road were expecting their first child.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives two specific examples of new knowledge.

questions.prompt: What did the child do to the books that the narrator found 'baffling and slightly charming'?
options: Removed all the books and stacked them on the ground
Arranged all the books in alphabetical order
Rearranged all the books so the spines faced inward
Added drawings to the covers of several books
questions.explanation: The narrator found that 'a child had rearranged all the books so that the spines faced inward.'
questions.hint: The action was unusual but not destructive.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe the change in pedestrian behaviour on the street after the library was installed?
options: People began to walk more slowly in general
People who used to hurry with heads down now slowed, looked, and occasionally stopped
Neighbours began holding regular outdoor events near the box
Children started playing on the street more often
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'People who once hurried past with their heads down now slowed, looked, occasionally stopped.'
questions.hint: The narrator contrasts old behaviour with new behaviour.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say the small wooden box achieved that larger community projects had not?
options: It raised money for neighbourhood improvements
It attracted visitors from other parts of the city
It made neighbours feel that they shared something
It encouraged people to read more regularly
questions.explanation: The narrator says the box 'made us feel that we shared something' — something larger projects had 'struggled to achieve.'
questions.hint: The narrator ends with this conclusion about the library's deeper impact.

cloze.template: A year ago, my neighbour Tom helped me bolt a small wooden box to a post on the corner of our street. It looked like a {{b1}} house — a tiny sloped roof, a glass door, and a hand-painted sign that read: Take a book, leave a book. We filled it with about thirty paperbacks from our own shelves, mostly novels and a few travel guides, and stepped back to see what would happen. I did not expect very much. I had been told that books would be stolen, or that the box would be vandalised, or that nobody would bother. What actually happened was quite different. Within the first week, we noticed that books were disappearing and being {{b2}} by others. Someone had left three science fiction novels I had never heard of. Someone else had {{b3}} a handwritten recipe card alongside a battered copy of a French cookbook, as if they wanted to share something personal. By the end of the first month, the little library had taken on a life of its {{b4}}. I started to pay attention to when people stopped at it. A teenage boy would come by most afternoons after school and spend five minutes choosing a book, sometimes putting one back and selecting another, with the same seriousness he might bring to any important decision. An older woman — I later learned her name was Margaret — had read every book in the box and replaced each one with two more. She told me it gave her something to do in the mornings that felt {{b5}}. What surprised me most was how the box became a {{b6}} for conversation. Neighbours who had lived on the same street for years began to stop and talk. I learned things about people I had never known — that the man three doors down had once been a journalist, that the young couple across the road were {{b7}} their first child. The library had created a reason for eye contact, for pausing, for noticing each other. It was not always {{b8}}. A few books disappeared without being replaced. Someone left a water-damaged paperback that nobody wanted. One morning I found that a child had rearranged all the books so that the {{b9}} faced inward, which I found both baffling and slightly charming. But these were minor things. The neighbourhood had quietly changed. People who once hurried past with their heads down now slowed, looked, occasionally stopped. A small wooden box had done what many larger community projects had {{b10}}: it had made us feel that we shared something.

blanks.explanation: 'Miniature' specifically means a small-scale version of something — perfect for describing a box that resembles a tiny house.
'Being replaced by others' — the passive construction shows that unknown visitors were performing the replacements.
'Tucked in' suggests something placed carefully and discretely within a small space — appropriate for a recipe card in a book box.
'Taken on a life of its own' is a fixed idiom meaning something has developed independently beyond its creator's control or expectations.
'Felt worthwhile' is the collocation — 'worthwhile' means valuable enough to justify the time or effort spent.
'Starting point for conversation' is the phrase used — it means the library provided an initial topic or location for interaction.
'Expecting a child' is the standard idiomatic expression for being pregnant — 'expecting their first child'.
'Seamless' means without any disruption or imperfection — the narrator says the experience was not always seamless, acknowledging small problems.
The 'spine' of a book is the narrow side that shows the title when shelved — 'spines faced inward' made the books unidentifiable.
'Struggled to achieve' means managed with great difficulty — it honours the effort while acknowledging the limited success of larger projects.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator suggests that a small, simple, and inexpensive idea — a box of free books — achieved what larger community projects had not. Why do you think small, informal gestures sometimes connect people more effectively than organised initiatives? What does this tell us about how community is built?
